question: [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker who observes a victim's signed Authorization in the mempool front-run it by submitting the identical calldata first, under the precondition that the victim has broadcast a valid (authorization, signature) pair with nonce == nonce[authorizer], triggering the call sequence attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) before victim, consuming nonce[authorizer]++, so victim's transaction reverts with InvalidNonce(), violating the invariant that a validly signed authorization with the correct nonce must be submittable by the authorizer, causing scoped impact: temporary griefing/freezing of the victim's ability to delegate authorization until they re-sign with nonce+1? Proof idea: fork-test with vm.prank(attacker) submitting victim's signed calldata first, assert victim's subsequent call reverts InvalidNonce and nonce[authorizer] == 1.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an already-authorized operator (isAuthorized[victim][operator] == true on Midnight) sign and submit a sequence of Authorization structs with consecutive nonces on behalf of the victim, under the precondition that operator holds a valid Midnight authorization for victim and EcrecoverAuthorizer is also authorized on Midnight for victim, triggering the call sequence operator signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=X, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N} and submits via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, consuming victim's nonces and revoking victim's existing delegations, violating the invariant that only the authorizer's own signed intent should consume their EcrecoverAuthorizer nonce, causing scoped impact: temporary freezing of victim's protocol access by revoking all operator authorizations? Proof idea: unit test where operator submits N revocation authorizations signed by operator (not victim), assert victim's pre-signed authorizations with nonces 0..N-1 all fail InvalidNonce.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the victim's authorization of a critical repay/withdraw operator near market maturity, under the precondition that operator is authorized on Midnight for victim and victim has an active debt position approaching maturity, triggering the call sequence operator signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=repayOperator, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N} and submits via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, then victim's repayOperator can no longer call Midnight.repay(market, units, victim, ...) on victim's behalf, violating the invariant that authorized operators must not be able to freeze a user's exit paths, causing scoped impact: temporary freezing of victim's debt repayment near maturity boundary, potentially triggering post-maturity liquidation? Proof idea: state test with market.maturity = block.timestamp + 1 hour, operator revokes repayOperator, assert repayOperator's repay call reverts Unauthorized.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a malicious address on behalf of the victim, under the precondition that operator is authorized on Midnight for victim (isAuthorized[victim][operator] == true) and EcrecoverAuthorizer is authorized on Midnight for victim, triggering the call sequence operator signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=malicious, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N} and submits via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, then malicious calls Midnight.withdrawCollateral(market, 0, amount, victim, malicious), violating the invariant that only the authorizer's own signed intent should expand their authorization set, causing scoped impact: temporary or permanent freezing/seizure of victim's collateral? Proof idea: unit test where operator grants malicious address authorization, assert malicious can withdraw victim's collateral.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator use EcrecoverAuthorizer to set consumed[victim][group] = type(uint256).max on behalf of victim, under the precondition that operator is authorized on Midnight for victim and victim has active offers in group, triggering the call sequence operator signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=operator, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N} then operator calls Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim), canceling all of victim's offers in that group, violating the invariant that offer cancellation must reflect maker intent, causing scoped impact: temporary griefing of victim's maker offers, preventing fills and freezing expected liquidity provision? Proof idea: unit test where operator cancels victim's offer group, assert subsequent take reverts due to consumed == max.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can a griefing attacker repeatedly submit Authorization structs with authorization.nonce = nonce[victim] but with a signature that recovers to address(0) or an unauthorized signer, under the precondition that the nonce check passes (nonce matches) but the signature check fails, triggering the call sequence attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with correct nonce but invalid sig, causing the transaction to revert after the nonce increment at line 26, violating the invariant that a failed authorization must not consume the nonce, causing scoped impact: nonce griefing if the nonce increment is not rolled back on revert? Proof idea: unit test asserting nonce[victim] is unchanged after a revert at the InvalidSignature check; confirm Solidity 0.8.34 reverts roll back the post-increment.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit EIP-712 signature malleability (flipping v between 27 and 28) to submit a victim's authorization twice before the victim's own submission, under the precondition that ecrecover accepts both v=27 and v=28 for the same (r,s) pair and both recover to the same authorizer address, triggering the call sequence attacker submits auth with v=27 consuming nonce N, then submits same auth with v=28 which fails InvalidNonce, then victim's original submission also fails InvalidNonce, violating the invariant that each signed authorization is usable exactly once by the authorizer, causing scoped impact: griefing of victim's authorization flow? Proof idea: fuzz v in {27, 28} for a fixed (r, s) and assert only one submission succeeds and nonce increments exactly once.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker grief a victim by submitting a valid authorization with authorization.deadline == block.timestamp (boundary), under the precondition that the victim signed an authorization with deadline == block.timestamp and the attacker submits it in the same block, triggering the call sequence attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with deadline == block.timestamp, which passes the require(block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline) check, consuming the nonce, violating the invariant that deadline-boundary authorizations should not be submittable by unauthorized parties, causing scoped impact: griefing of victim's authorization timing assumptions? Proof idea: unit test with vm.warp(deadline) and assert submission succeeds at exactly deadline.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke EcrecoverAuthorizer's own authorization on Midnight for the victim, under the precondition that operator is authorized on Midnight for victim and EcrecoverAuthorizer is authorized on Midnight for victim, triggering the call sequence operator signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=EcrecoverAuthorizer, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N} and submits via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, which calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(EcrecoverAuthorizer, false, victim), then all future EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized calls for victim will revert at Midnight.setIsAuthorized because isAuthorized[victim][EcrecoverAuthorizer] == false, violating the invariant that the authorization mechanism itself must not be self-disableable by an operator, causing scoped impact: permanent freezing of victim's ability to use EcrecoverAuthorizer for signature-based delegation? Proof idea: unit test where operator self-revokes EcrecoverAuthorizer, assert subsequent EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized for victim reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use Midnight.multicall to atomically revoke a victim's operator authorization and then take a position on behalf of the victim in the same transaction, under the precondition that attacker is authorized on Midnight for victim and victim has collateral deposited, triggering the call sequence attacker calls Midnight.multicall([setIsAuthorized(repayOperator, false, victim), take(offer, ..., victim, ...)]), revoking repayOperator and increasing victim's debt in one atomic call, violating the invariant that authorization changes and position changes must not be composable to trap a user in an unexitable position, causing scoped impact: temporary freezing of victim's debt repayment path? Proof idea: multicall integration test asserting repayOperator cannot repay after the multicall.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a new address and then immediately use that address to call Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim) to cancel victim's offers, under the precondition that operator is authorized on Midnight for victim, triggering the call sequence operator signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=newAddr, isAuthorized=true}, submits via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, then newAddr calls Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim), violating the invariant that offer cancellation must reflect maker intent and not be achievable by a chain of authorized delegates, causing scoped impact: griefing of victim's active offers and partial-fill state? Proof idea: unit test asserting newAddr can cancel victim's offers after being granted authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker grief a victim by submitting the victim's valid signed authorization with a manipulated authorization.authorized field (different from what victim intended), under the precondition that the attacker cannot forge the signature but can observe the victim's signed authorization in the mempool and submit it with the original authorized address, triggering the call sequence attacker submits victim's authorization granting authorization to attacker-controlled address, consuming victim's nonce, violating the invariant that the authorized address in the submitted authorization must match victim's intent, causing scoped impact: griefing by consuming victim's nonce and granting authorization to an unintended address? Proof idea: confirm that the signature commits to the authorized field via AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH and assert a modified authorized field causes Unauthorized revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to themselves for a second Midnight instance (if multiple Midnight deployments exist), under the precondition that EcrecoverAuthorizer.MIDNIGHT points to Midnight_A but the operator wants to abuse authorization on Midnight_B, triggering the call sequence operator signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=operator, isAuthorized=true} for EcrecoverAuthorizer_A, submits it, then uses the resulting isAuthorized[victim][operator] on Midnight_A to call Midnight_A.setIsAuthorized(operator, true, victim) on Midnight_B if Midnight_B shares the same authorization mapping, violating the invariant that authorization must be scoped to the correct Midnight instance, causing scoped impact: cross-instance authorization confusion? Proof idea: deploy two Midnight instances and two EcrecoverAuthorizers, assert authorization on one does not affect the other.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker grief a victim by submitting a valid authorization with authorization.isAuthorized=false for an operator the victim has not yet authorized, under the precondition that victim has nonce=0 and has not yet used EcrecoverAuthorizer, triggering the call sequence attacker obtains victim's signed Authorization{authorized=X, isAuthorized=false, nonce=0} (e.g., from a test environment or replay), submits it via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, consuming nonce 0, so victim's intended authorization with nonce=0 fails InvalidNonce, violating the invariant that a user's first EcrecoverAuthorizer interaction must not be consumable by a replayed or front-run revocation, causing scoped impact: griefing of victim's initial authorization setup? Proof idea: unit test where attacker submits a revocation auth signed by victim with nonce=0, assert victim's subsequent grant auth with nonce=0 reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a contract that, when called as a ratifier, always returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS, effectively bypassing offer ratification for the victim's offers, under the precondition that operator is authorized on Midnight for victim and victim uses EcrecoverRatifier for their offers, triggering the call sequence operator signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=maliciousRatifier, isAuthorized=true} and submits via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, then maliciousRatifier can ratify any offer on behalf of victim, violating the invariant that ratifier authorization must reflect maker intent, causing scoped impact: griefing of victim's offer security by allowing unauthorized offer fills? Proof idea: unit test where maliciousRatifier is authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer and can ratify victim's offers.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker grief a victim by submitting a valid authorization with authorization.authorized == authorization.authorizer (self-authorization), under the precondition that victim signed such an authorization (e.g., for testing), triggering the call sequence attacker submits Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=victim, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N} via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, consuming nonce N, so victim's intended authorization with nonce=N fails InvalidNonce, violating the invariant that self-authorization submissions must not consume nonces in a way that blocks other pending authorizations, causing scoped impact: griefing of victim's authorization queue? Proof idea: unit test asserting self-authorization consumes nonce and subsequent authorization with same nonce reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the victim's authorization of their liquidation-protection operator near maturity, under the precondition that victim has an unhealthy position and relies on an authorized operator to repay debt before liquidation, triggering the call sequence operator signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=protectionOperator, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N} and submits via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, then protectionOperator cannot call Midnight.repay(market, units, victim, ...) on victim's behalf, violating the invariant that authorized operators must not be able to freeze a user's liquidation-protection mechanisms, causing scoped impact: temporary freezing of victim's debt repayment, enabling liquidation of victim's position? Proof idea: state test where operator revokes protectionOperator, assert protectionOperator's repay reverts Unauthorized and victim becomes liquidatable.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker grief a victim by submitting a valid authorization with authorization.deadline == type(uint256).max, under the precondition that victim signed such an authorization for convenience, triggering the call sequence attacker front-runs victim's submission with the same calldata, consuming nonce N, so victim's transaction fails InvalidNonce, violating the invariant that long-lived authorizations must not be more susceptible to front-running griefing than short-lived ones, causing scoped impact: griefing of victim's authorization with no deadline protection? Proof idea: unit test asserting that a max-deadline authorization can be front-run and consumed by any caller.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a contract that calls Midnight.withdrawCollateral on behalf of the victim during a callback, under the precondition that operator is authorized on Midnight for victim and victim has collateral deposited, triggering the call sequence operator grants authorization to maliciousContract via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, then maliciousContract is used as a takerCallback in a take call, and during the callback maliciousContract calls Midnight.withdrawCollateral(market, 0, amount, victim, maliciousContract), violating the invariant that collateral withdrawal must require explicit victim authorization at the time of withdrawal, causing scoped impact: temporary freezing or seizure of victim's collateral via callback reentrancy? Proof idea: integration test where maliciousContract withdraws victim's collateral during a take callback after being authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker grief a victim by submitting a valid authorization with authorization.nonce == nonce[victim] but with authorization.authorized == address(0), under the precondition that victim has not signed such an authorization but the attacker has obtained a signature for a different authorization with the same nonce, triggering the call sequence attacker submits Authorization{authorized=address(0), isAuthorized=true, nonce=N} with a valid signature, consuming nonce N, so victim's intended authorization with nonce=N fails InvalidNonce, violating the invariant that authorizations with address(0) as authorized must not be submittable or must not consume nonces, causing scoped impact: griefing of victim's authorization queue? Proof idea: unit test asserting that an authorization with authorized=address(0) consumes the nonce and subsequent authorizations with the same nonce revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a new address and then use that address to call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(anotherAddress, true, victim), creating an unbounded authorization chain, under the precondition that operator is authorized on Midnight for victim, triggering the call sequence operator grants newAddr via EcrecoverAuthorizer, newAddr grants anotherAddr via Midnight.setIsAuthorized, anotherAddr grants yet another address, creating a chain of N authorized addresses all with full control over victim's Midnight state, violating the invariant that authorization delegation must be bounded and auditable, causing scoped impact: griefing of victim's authorization state through unbounded delegation chains? Proof idea: unit test asserting that a chain of N delegations all have full control over victim's collateral and credit.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker grief a victim by submitting a valid authorization that grants authorization to a contract that will revert on all Midnight calls, under the precondition that victim signed an authorization for a smart contract operator that has a bug causing it to revert, triggering the call sequence attacker submits victim's signed authorization granting authorization to buggyContract, consuming nonce N, then victim cannot revoke this authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer (nonce N+1 required) if the buggyContract also controls nonce consumption, violating the invariant that authorization grants must not create irrevocable states, causing scoped impact: temporary freezing of victim's ability to manage their authorization set? Proof idea: unit test asserting victim can still directly call Midnight.setIsAuthorized to revoke buggyContract without using EcrecoverAuthorizer.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator use EcrecoverAuthorizer to manipulate the victim's collateral bitmap by granting authorization to a contract that deposits dust collateral across all MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER slots, under the precondition that operator is authorized on Midnight for victim and victim has an active debt position, triggering the call sequence operator grants dustDepositor via EcrecoverAuthorizer, dustDepositor calls Midnight.supplyCollateral(market, i, 1 wei, victim) for each collateral index i up to MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER, filling victim's bitmap, violating the invariant that collateral bitmap limits must not be reachable by unauthorized parties, causing scoped impact: temporary freezing of victim's ability to add new collateral types? Proof idea: unit test asserting that after bitmap is full, victim cannot add a new collateral type.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker grief a victim by submitting a valid authorization with a deadline exactly at market maturity, under the precondition that victim signed an authorization with deadline == market.maturity and the attacker submits it at block.timestamp == market.maturity, triggering the call sequence attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized at block.timestamp == market.maturity, which passes the deadline check, consuming nonce N, and the resulting authorization change affects victim's ability to repay or withdraw at maturity, violating the invariant that maturity-boundary authorization changes must not interfere with settlement flows, causing scoped impact: temporary freezing of victim's maturity settlement? Proof idea: state test with vm.warp(market.maturity) and assert authorization change at maturity boundary affects repay/withdraw.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Temporary freezing or griefing of user funds through dust, rounding, maturity boundary, bitmap limit, or partial-fill edge cases'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a contract that partially fills victim's offers and then cancels the remaining capacity, under the precondition that operator is authorized on Midnight for victim and victim has active offers with partial fills, triggering the call sequence operator grants partialFillGriever via EcrecoverAuthorizer, partialFillGriever calls Midnight.setConsumed(group, partialAmount, victim) to advance consumed state, then calls Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim) to cancel remaining capacity, violating the invariant that partial-fill state must reflect maker intent and not be manipulable by authorized delegates, causing scoped impact: griefing of victim's offer partial-fill accounting? Proof idea: unit test asserting partialFillGriever can advance and then cancel victim's offer consumption.
